quiz.title: SAT Mathematics: Unit 9 Quiz 8
quiz.subtitle: Probability: Advanced Contexts
quiz.subject: SAT Preparation
quiz.topic: Conditional probability, combinatorics, and independent events

questions.question: A quality control manager at a factory finds that the probability of a part being defective is $0.02$. If two parts are chosen at random, what is the probability that at least one of the parts is NOT defective?
questions.explanation: The probability that 'at least one is not defective' is the complement of the probability that 'both are defective.' The probability both are defective is $0.02 \times 0.02 = 0.0004$. The probability at least one is not defective is $1 - 0.0004 = 0.9996$.

questions.question: A high school offers two elective courses: Photography and Graphic Design. $40\%$ of the students take Photography, $30\%$ take Graphic Design, and $10\%$ take both. If a student is selected at random and it is known they take Photography, what is the probability they also take Graphic Design?
questions.explanation: This is a conditional probability $P(G|P) = \frac{P(G \text{ and } P)}{P(P)}$. Substituting the values gives $0.10 / 0.40 = 0.25$.

questions.question: A jar contains 10 pieces of candy: 4 are chocolate and 6 are peppermint. If three pieces are selected one by one without replacement, what is the probability that all three pieces are peppermint?
questions.explanation: The probability is calculated as the product of the individual probabilities for each draw: $(6/10) \times (5/9) \times (4/8) = 120/720 = 1/6$.

questions.question: A set of cards consists of 5 red cards and 5 black cards. A student draws a card, notes the color, and returns it to the deck. The student repeats this 3 times. What is the probability that exactly 2 of the 3 cards drawn are red?
questions.explanation: Since the card is replaced, the probability of red on any draw is $1/2$. The possible sequences for exactly 2 red (R) and 1 black (B) are RRB, RBR, and BRR. Each sequence has a probability of $(1/2)^3 = 1/8$. Since there are 3 such sequences, the total probability is $3 \times (1/8) = 3/8$.

questions.question:
A survey of 120 people asked whether they preferred tea or coffee. The results are shown below.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Preferred Coffee} & \text{Preferred Tea} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{Under 30} & 45 & 15 & 60 \\ \hline \text{30 and Over} & 30 & 30 & 60 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 75 & 45 & 120 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

If a person who prefers tea is selected at random, what is the probability that they are under 30 years old?
questions.explanation: Identify the 'Preferred Tea' column total (45). Out of those 45 people, 15 are under 30. The probability is $15/45 = 1/3$.

questions.question: A weather forecaster states that the probability of rain on Saturday is $40\%$ and the probability of rain on Sunday is $25\%$. If the occurrence of rain on these two days is independent, what is the probability that it rains on at least one of the two days?
questions.explanation: To find the probability of 'at least one,' calculate $1 - P(\text{no rain on either day})$. The probability of no rain on Saturday is $0.60$ and no rain on Sunday is $0.75$. $P(\text{no rain}) = 0.60 \times 0.75 = 0.45$. Thus, $P(\text{at least one rain}) = 1 - 0.45 = 0.55$, or $55\%$.

questions.question: A committee of 3 people is to be chosen from a group of 5 men and 2 women. What is the probability that the committee will consist of 2 men and 1 woman?
questions.explanation: Total ways to choose 3 from 7: $\binom{7}{3} = \frac{7 \times 6 \times 5}{3 \times 2 \times 1} = 35$. Ways to choose 2 men from 5: $\binom{5}{2} = 10$. Ways to choose 1 woman from 2: $\binom{2}{1} = 2$. Total favorable outcomes: $10 \times 2 = 20$. Probability: $20/35 = 4/7$.

questions.question:
The table below categorizes 80 historical artifacts by their material and the era they originated from.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Stone} & \text{Bronze} & \text{Iron} \\ \hline \text{Era X} & 12 & 8 & 4 \\ \hline \text{Era Y} & 6 & 14 & 36 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

If an artifact from Era Y is selected at random, what is the probability that it is made of Bronze?
questions.explanation: Identify the total for Era Y: $6 + 14 + 36 = 56$. The number of Bronze artifacts in Era Y is 14. The probability is $14/56 = 1/4$ or $7/28$.

questions.question: A game involves rolling a fair four-sided die (numbered 1–4) and tossing a fair coin. What is the probability that the die lands on an even number AND the coin lands on heads?
questions.explanation: Probability of an even number (2 or 4) on a four-sided die: $2/4 = 1/2$. Probability of heads: $1/2$. Since the events are independent, multiply the probabilities: $(1/2) \times (1/2) = 1/4$.

questions.question: In a library, $70\%$ of the books are fiction and $30\%$ are non-fiction. Of the fiction books, $20\%$ are hardcovers. Of the non-fiction books, $50\%$ are hardcovers. If a randomly selected book is a hardcover, what is the probability it is non-fiction?
questions.explanation: Assume there are 100 books. Fiction Hardcovers: $0.70 \times 0.20 = 14$. Non-fiction Hardcovers: $0.30 \times 0.50 = 15$. Total hardcovers: $14 + 15 = 29$. The probability that a hardcover is non-fiction is $15/29$.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent work! You can confidently calculate conditional probabilities, apply combinatorics to probability problems, and handle multi-step independent event calculations.
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review missed questions and focus on distinguishing between 'at least one' (use complement) and 'exactly n' (count favorable sequences), and remember that conditional probability restricts the sample space.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Remember: 'at least one not' = 1 - P(all are), conditional probability = P(A and B)/P(B), and for binomial situations identify n, k, and p carefully.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Start by drawing tree diagrams for multi-step events, label probabilities at each branch, and practice identifying whether events are independent or dependent before calculating.
